coredata.dc:title: N-CQDs from reed straw enriching charge over BiO2−x/BiOCl p-n heterojunction for improved visible-light-driven photodegradation of organic pollutants
coredata.dc:creator: Qi, Kemin
Ye, Yuping
Wei, Bin
Li, Mengxin
Lun, Yanxin
Xie, Xiaoyun
Xie, Haijiao
coredata.dc:description: Green bismuth-based photocatalysts have attracted extensive attention in the field of PPCPs photodegradation. The improved carrier separation efficiency still remains a key factor to enhance photocatalytic performance. Herein, N-doped biomass carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) decorated p-n heterojunction photocatalyst BiO2−x/BiOCl was prepared using a facile ion-etching strategy, and it displayed a markedly enhanced catalytic activity in the photodegradation of sulfonamide antibiotics. Calculated by the differential charge density, the doped N-CQDs could gather photogenerated electrons, which indicated that the introduction of N-CQDs into BiO2−x/BiOCl would effectively inhibit the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers. In addition, photocatalytic performance and density functional theory (DFT) calculation results revealed that the photogenerated electrons tended to transfer from p-BiOCl to n-BiO2−x through N-CQDs, which could generate ·O2 - and photogenerated h+ to oxidize the target pollutants. Benefiting from the synergistic effect of accelerated separation of e--h+ in p-n heterojunction and the electron-rich performance of N-CQDs, the superb TOC removal efficiencies (89.40% within 120 min visible-light irradiation) and toxicity reduction performance of photodegradation intermediates were achieved. As a consequence, this work will provide a design of high-quality photocatalysts and a green-promising strategy for bismuth-based photocatalysts in the water treatment of PPCPs.

head.ce:title: N-CQDs from reed straw enriching charge over BiO2−x/BiOCl p-n heterojunction for improved visible-light-driven photodegradation of organic pollutants

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Green bismuth-based photocatalysts have attracted extensive attention in the field of PPCPs photodegradation. The improved carrier separation efficiency still remains a key factor to enhance photocatalytic performance. Herein, N-doped biomass carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) decorated p-n heterojunction photocatalyst BiO2−x/BiOCl was prepared using a facile ion-etching strategy, and it displayed a markedly enhanced catalytic activity in the photodegradation of sulfonamide antibiotics. Calculated by the differential charge density, the doped N-CQDs could gather photogenerated electrons, which indicated that the introduction of N-CQDs into BiO2−x/BiOCl would effectively inhibit the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers. In addition, photocatalytic performance and density functional theory (DFT) calculation results revealed that the photogenerated electrons tended to transfer from p-BiOCl to n-BiO2−x through N-CQDs, which could generate ·O2 - and photogenerated h+ to oxidize the target pollutants. Benefiting from the synergistic effect of accelerated separation of e--h+ in p-n heterojunction and the electron-rich performance of N-CQDs, the superb TOC removal efficiencies (89.40% within 120 min visible-light irradiation) and toxicity reduction performance of photodegradation intermediates were achieved. As a consequence, this work will provide a design of high-quality photocatalysts and a green-promising strategy for bismuth-based photocatalysts in the water treatment of PPCPs.

ce:section.ce:para: The existence of pharmaceutical and personal care products (PPCPs) in the aquatic environment has become an emerging and serious environmental issue that scientific researchers are trying to solve. Among the common PPCPs, the detection rate of antibiotics in the aquatic environment is higher than others. Sulfonamide antibiotics (SAs) are widely used for disease control, since the metabolism of humans and animals and the incomplete treatment of sewage plants, a large amount of SAs have been discharged into the aquatic environment (Chen et al., 2013; Ji et al., 2014., Bonvin et al., 2013). Seriously, because of their persistence and universality, the SAs in the environmental waters may induce antibiotic resistance genes, which poses an enormous threat to aquatic organisms and human health (Miao et al., 2004). Thus, how to effectively and safely remove SAs from environmental waters is an urgent issue to be solved by researchers.

Compared with water remediation technologies such as precipitation, membrane filtration, and biological treatment, photocatalysis is an emerging and promising advanced oxidation process (AOPs) with great potential in addressing environmental issues (Kumar et al., 2020; Hasija et al., 2019; Sudhaik et al., 2018). It can be carried out under mild conditions without additional oxidants to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) by oxidizing H2O molecules and activating O2 molecules (Loeb et al., 2019). Also, the photocatalytic technology can better utilize and convert green and sustainable solar energy, which possesses many applications in pollutant degradation in wastewater, air pollution control, hydrogen fuel production, and carbon dioxide reduction (Ganose et al., 2016). However, due to the low photocatalytic reaction efficiency of monomeric photocatalysts, there is an urgent need for catalysts with better photocatalytic performance. Heterojunction is an interface between two different photocatalysts with different band structures. When the heterojunction was excited by light irradiation, the photocatalytic reaction could happen at the interface between the adjacent photocatalysts. Thence, heterogeneous photocatalysis has excellent light harvesting ability, efficient carrier separation ability, and stability. which could display great potential in the field of pollutant photodegradation (Raizada et al., 2021; Kumar et al., 2021).

Bismuth oxychloride (BiOCl), a typical and valuable p-type semiconductor, is consisted of [Bi2O2]2+-[Cl2]2- layers (Wang et al., 2018) The BiOCl has drawn much attention in the photocatalysis field attributed to its low-toxicity, stabilized chemical property, unique optical property, and high earth abundance (Kuila et al., 2021; Gao et al., 2019). However, the BiOCl is difficult to be excited under visible-light and its activity and photodegradation efficiency are low because of the wide bandgap (~3.2 eV) and positive conduction band position (Kato et al., 2017) In order to achieve better photocatalytic performance for enhancing the optical absorption and separating the photo-generated e- and h+ under visible light, BiOCl-based heterojunction constructing may be an effective strategy, which can narrow the bandgap and boost the interfacial charge transfer, resulting in higher photocatalytic performance under visible light irradiation.

Due to the effect of the interfacial charge transfer, semiconductor photocatalysts with p-n heterojunction usually convert solar energy to chemical energy more effectively than single-phase semiconductors (Dona et al., 2021). When two semiconductors with different band positions are in contact, an internal electronic field can drive the electrons to the n-type semiconductor conduction band (CB), while the holes to the valence band (VB) of the p-type semiconductor. P-n heterojunction with the spatial configuration of band potential, which can be the key factor to induce the advanced electron transferring capacity. The p-n heterojunction system can help to create internal electricity between the surfaces of two semiconducting photocatalysts and then accelerate the separation of the photo-generated electrons and holes, accordingly obtaining high photocatalytic performance (Rubino et al., 2021). Among the semiconducting photocatalyst, BiO2−x is an emerging n-type semiconductor with a narrow bandgap (< 2.0 eV), excellent visible-light response (> 850 nm), and rich oxygen vacancies (Li et al., 2018a). Thus, BiOCl and BiO2−x with suitable band-gap structures can improve the photocatalytic performance through constructing the p-n heterojunction is considered. However, the rapid separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs is essential but not sufficient to achieve high-performance photocatalysts. It is worthwhile to modify BiO2−x/BiOCl to improve the photo-response of photocatalyst and the stability of the crystal structure to further improve the photocatalytic ability and reusability (Al-Kahtani et al., 2019; Ahamad et al., 2020).

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) could be a promising electron transfer intermediary candidate because of their low toxicity, high chemical stability, photo-generated electron transfer, and excellent up-conversion luminescence abilities, which has attracted growing attention (Yan et al., 2015). CQDs can also be used as photosensitizers to enhance the ability to capture light, thereby broadening the response range of the composite photocatalyst in visible light (Wang et al., 2014a). Compared with the carbon source precursors of toxic chemical agents, the use of waste biomass as a carbon source is more environmental-friendly (Qi et al., 2022). According to the relevant studies, interactions of unsaturated bonds on the surfaces of the CQDs with heteroatoms, such as N and Cl atoms, could produce new electronic states in the band structure and further affect the photo-generated charge carrier separation and recombination (Murali et al., 2021; Yousaf et al., 2021). Due to the electron-rich nature of N atoms, N doping on CQDs can increase the HOMO energy, and further significantly reduce the gap of LUMO-HOMO of CQDs, resulting in a redshift of emission to improve the optical property of CQDs (Liu et al., 2019) In addition, N-doped can effectively improve their luminescence performance and prolong the lifetime of charge carriers to boost the photocatalytic ability due to the introduction of the amino group (-NH2) (Yu et al., 2020). The doping of N-CQDs can enhance the internal electric field of the p-n heterojunction, promote carrier migration, and reduce the recombination of carriers during the migration process.

In this work, BiO2−x/BiOCl p-n heterojunctions decorated with N-CQDs were synthesized to distinctly heighten the photodegradation of sulfamethoxazole (SMX) under visible light. Firstly, the crystal phase, morphology, surface chemical composition, optical and photoelectrochemical properties of N-CQDs/BiO2−x/BiOCl (BBN) were studied systematically. Secondly, in combination with practical applications, SAs were chosen as the target pollutant to investigate the photocatalytic performance of BBN under visible-light irradiation. Thirdly, the quenching experiments and density functional theory (DFT) calculation about the density of states (DOS), work function, and charge density differences were employed to propose detailed and plausible SMX degradation mechanisms and pathways. Finally, the eco-toxicity of SMX and its photodegradation intermediates through the BBN photocatalysis system were evaluated by utilizing the ECOSAR program.
The detailed information of reagents of synthesized photocatalysts are shown in Text S1 of Supplementary Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-doped reed straw biomass carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The urea and reed straw biomass were used as the nitrogen source and carbon source, respectively, green N-CQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal methods based on previous literature (Wang et al., 2017; Xie et al., 2021). 0.5 g reed straw biomass powder was dispersed into 10 mL 0.1 M urea solution with vigorously magnetic stirring for 1 h. Next, the mixed solution was transferred to a 30 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 ℃ for 3 h. After cooling it to room temperature, collecting the brown suspension through a centrifugal process and dialyzing against ultrapure water to get rid of impurities through a dialysis membrane for 24 h (MWCO: 1000 Da). The obtained N-CQDs solution was sealed at 4 ℃, away from light.

BO was prepared by the typical one-step hydrothermal method. At first, 8 mmol NaBiO3·2H2O was dispersed in 70 mL distilled water. The suspension was sonicated and stirred until the NaBiO3·2H2O was evenly dispersed. Then it was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave at 160 ℃ for 8 h. The brown precipitates were collected and washed with ethanol and distilled water several times and dried at 60 ℃. A mechanistic approach for the generation of defects through the hydrothermal method to confirm the generation of BiO2−x was proposed in Text S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of BiOCl (BOC), BiO2−x/BiOCl (BB) and N-CQDs/BiO2−x/BiOCl (BBN)
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the previous literature, the BOC was prepared by the ion-etching method (Chai et al., 2009). 0.482 g BO powder was dispersed in 20 mL distilled water, then 1 M HCl solution (6 mL) was added slowly while the suspension was vigorously stirred. The resultant suspension was stirred for 4 h and then the obtained BOC was collected through a centrifugal process. The produced white precipitation was washed with distilled water and ethanol several times and then dried at 60 ℃.

Photocatalyst BB was prepared using a similar method with BOC but adding 1 mL 1 M HCl to the suspension with vigorously stirring. For BBN, 0.482 g BO powder and 1 mL N-CQDs were dispersed together in 20 mL distilled water, then 1 mL 1 M HCl solution was added dropwise while the suspension was vigorously stirred. The produced BB and BBN were washed with ethanol and distilled water several times and then dried at 60 ℃.
The detailed characterization methods of synthesized photocatalysts are shown in Text S3 of Supplementary Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity tests and photoelectrochemical measurements
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic activities of as-prepared photocatalysts were evaluated by the photodegradation of sulfamethoxazole (SMX, >98%, J&K Scientific Company of China) under 50 W LED visible light irradiation (dominant wavelength was 475 nm and Fig. S1 showed the detailed wavelength range of LED lamp). In addition, the photocatalytic measurement of BBN composites was also performed under natural sunlight irradiation (N36°03′, E103°40′, Lanzhou, China, the sunlight intensity at that time was tested by FZ-A radiometer and the result was 48.2 mW/cm2) in this work. After adding 40 mg photocatalyst to SMX solution (10 mg/L, 50 mL), the suspension was kept stirring in the dark for 30 min to achieve the adsorption-desorption equilibrium and then the LED lamp was turned on (The adsorption-desorption equilibrium kinetics were shown in the Fig S2). Within a certain interval of time, the suspension was collected and filtered through 0.22 µm membrane filters for further analysis. The above experiments were implemented two times and the data were presented in the form of mean and standard deviation.

Notably, the photocatalytic performance of as-prepared photocatalysts (BO, BOC, BB, BBN) was assessed under the same reaction conditions. The dosage (0.2 g/L~1.0 g/L), natural organic matter (HA), inorganic species (Cl-, SO4 2-, NO3 -), and other sulfonamide antibiotics (Sulfamethazine: SM2, >99%, J&K Scientific Company of China; Sulfadiazine: SD, >99%, J&K Scientific Company of China; Sulfathiazole: ST, >98%, Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd.; Sulfamonomethoxine: SMM, >93%, Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd.; Sulfadimethoxine: SDM, >98%, Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd.; Sulfamerazine: SMR, >98%, Alfa Aesar) were also studied to determine the application potential of BBN in an aqueous solution.

The photoelectrochemical tests of as-prepared BO, BOC, BB, and BBN were tested through CS350H electrochemical workstation (Wuhan CorrTest Instruments Corp. Ltd). And the detailed sample preparation and photoelectrochemical measurements were shown in Text S4 of Supplementary Material.

Analytical methods of SMX concentration, detailed density functional theory (DFT) calculations methods, and eco-toxicity of SMX photodegradation intermediates were demonstrated in Text S5 of Supplementary Material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and surface morphology of photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: XRD patterns were used to study the phase structure and crystallinity of BO, BOC, BB, and BBN. As shown in Fig. 1, the diffraction patterns of pure BO at 2θ values of 14.10°, 32.69°. 46.91°, 55.64°, 75.64°, and 77.98° were corresponded to (1 1 1), (2 0 0), (2 2 0), (3 1 1), (3 3 1), and (4 2 0) planes of cubic BiO2−x (pdf#47-1057), respectively. The typical diffraction peaks at 11.98°, 25.86°, 32.49°, 33.45°, 40.89°, 46.63°, 54.09°, and 58.60° were indexed to (0 0 1), (1 0 1), (1 1 0), (1 0 2), (1 1 2), (2 0 0), (2 1 1), and (2 1 2) planes of tetragonal structure BiOCl (pdf#06–0249). In addition, because of the presence of highly disordered carbon atoms in N-CQDs, the XRD pattern of it showed a broad peak centered at 25° (Fig. S3) (Zhu et al., 2013). The XRD patterns of BB and BBN consisted of two sets of diffraction peaks of BO and BOC, which further confirmed that the coexistence of BO and BOC in the BB and BBN photocatalysts. Nevertheless, there was no obvious diffraction peak of N-CQDs observed in the XRD patterns of BBN, which might be due to the small introduction content and disordered structure of N-CQDs. Meanwhile, there were no characteristic peaks from other impurity phases were found in the as-prepared samples, indicating that the synthesized BBN photocatalyst had high purity.

Raman spectra are often used to detect the purity and the structure of the composite with high sensitivity shown in Fig. S4. The Raman spectra of BOC, BO, BB, and BBN photocatalysts further verified the presence of BOC and BO in the composite. The characteristic peaks at 95 cm−1, 149 cm−1, and 206 cm−1 belonged to the pure BOC, and the 149 cm-1 and 206 cm−1 were assigned to the A1g internal Bi-Cl and Eg internal Bi-Cl stretching mode, respectively (Shamaila et al., 2011). In addition, the peaks at around 145 cm−1, 311 cm−1, 487 cm−1, and 615 cm−1 were attributed to pure BO (Hao et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2019).

The morphological feature and element distribution of obtained composites were examined by SEM, TEM, and EDS-mapping as shown in Fig. 2 and Fig. S5. Pure BO presented a rod structure with a rough surface and the BOC exhibited the nanosheet morphology (Fig. 2(a and b)). When BB was formed by ion etching of BO, it could be observed that BOC nanosheets were dispersed on the surface of rod-shaped BO (Fig. 2(c)). Interestingly, similar morphology was observed on the ternary photocatalyst BBN (Fig. 2(d)) but the BOC nanosheets formed were thinner and more uniform than that of BB. Generally, the hierarchical layered structure could produce multiple reflections of light, thus, which could improve the light-harvesting capacity (Wu et al., 2016b). Compared with BB, there were no significant images of N-CQDs in BBN due to the small size of N-CQDs and the limited resolution of SEM. Moreover, the EDS-mapping image presented in Fig. S5 that Bi, O, Cl, C, and N were evenly distributed in the BBN, which could be further inferred that the introduction of N-CQDs in BBN was successful.

To further study morphologies and observe the distribution of N-CQDs onto the surfaces of BBN, TEM images of N-CQDs and BBN were gained as displayed in Fig. 2(e-f). As shown in Fig. 2(e), black dots with a size of 10 nm could be clearly discovered as biomass N-CQDs. Besides, the HRTEM images of the N-CQDs revealed the presence of graphite carbon crystalline lattice with a spacing of 0.22 nm, which corresponded to (1 0 0) face of bulk graphite (Fig. 2(f)), indicating that N-CQDs successfully synthesized (Qi et al., 2022). BOC nanosheets were mutually accumulated on the rod-like BO could be found from the TEM image of BBN in Fig. 2(g). In the HRTEM of BBN (Fig. 2(h)), N-CQDS were discovered (marked with yellow circle) on the composite photocatalyst. Besides, the clear lattice spacing could be observed, and the spacing of 0.322 nm, 0.275 nm, and 0.22 nm belong to (1 1 1), (1 1 0), and (1 0 0) facets of BO, BOC, and N-CQDs (Fig S6), respectively, which determined the great combination of above photocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functional groups and surface chemical composition of photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: FT-IR spectrums of N-CQDs and BBN were depicted in Fig. S7. N-CQDs contained rich chemical bonds and functional groups, where the characteristic peaks located at 1114.3 and 1157.2 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of -C-O-C-, oxidative functional group, and -C-NH-C-, respectively (Yan et al., 2015; Gao et al., 2021). The appearance of peaks at 1401.1 cm−1 and 1598.3 cm−1 were accorded with the C-N and N-H adsorption bands (Wang et al., 2014b). An obvious increase appeared at 2929.5 cm−1 corresponding to stretching vibrations of C-H. In addition, a broad strong band in the range of 3200–3700 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibration of N-H and O-H bonds. Hence, the analysis of FT-IR showed a variety of functional groups such as amines, alcohols, carbonyls, and aromatic rings existed on the N-CQDs surface, which derived from straw biomass and urea. And the presence of these functional groups such as -OH and -COOH on the surface made N-CQDs a highly water-soluble ability (Yan et al., 2015; Bie et al., 2019). As for composite photocatalyst BBN, the peak at 544 cm−1 belonged to the symmetric stretching vibration of Bi-O bond (Hu et al., 2019). However, the peaks of N-CQDs were distinctly weakened and disappeared in the spectrum of BBN, which might be due to the carboxyl group of N-CQDs combined with Bi atom to form a situ growth site, indicating composite photocatalyst BBN fabrication was successful (Wu et al., 2016b; Bie et al., 2019).

To further analyze the surface chemical state and electronic state of BBN composite structure, the XPS analysis was performed in Fig. 3. According to XPS wide spectrum (Fig. 3(a)), the detected elements consisted of the relevant composition of N-CQDs, BO, BOC, and BBN. In addition, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of detailed elements were shown in Fig. 3(b-f). Specifically, the binding energy of 158.50/159.45 eV and 163.65/164.35 eV corresponded to the Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2, respectively, which were ascribed to the mixed-valence states of Bi (Ⅲ, Ⅴ)-O bonds in the BO and BBN. In detail, the binding energy of 158.50 eV and 163.65 eV were assigned to Bi (Ⅲ), and the 159.45 eV and 164.35 eV were attributed to Bi (Ⅴ) (Li et al., 2018b). Comparatively, for BOC, there were two additional peaks centered at 159.45 eV and 164.80 eV that appeared in XPS spectra of Bi 4f corresponded to Bi (Ⅲ). The high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1s fitted well to three different characteristic peaks at 529.35 eV, 530.95 eV, and 532.40 eV (Gao et al., 2019). The O 1s binding energy of 529.35 eV and 530.95 eV were assigned to the lattice binding energy with Bi-O and oxygen vacancy regions, respectively, while the characteristic peak at 532.40 eV in the spectra was attributed to the physically and chemically surface-absorbed oxygen species (Li et al., 2018a, 2018b). Especially, there was an obvious oxygen-vacancy peak at 530.95 eV of O 1s assured in pure BO, which indicated that BO with oxygen-vacancy defects was successfully prepared. The binding energy of 197.79 eV and 199.34 eV were related to the characteristic peak of Cl 2p, which indexed to Cl- in BBN (Wang et al., 2018).

The N 1s spectrum in Fig. 3(e) displayed two peaks at 399.40 eV and 400.07 eV were accorded with N-H and CN bonds in the N-CQDs (Wang et al., 2017; Zhu et al., 2013). The characteristic peaks at 283.87 eV, 285.26 eV, and 286.20 eV in the C 1s XPS spectrum were attributed to the graphitic (CC and C-C), C-N, and C-O bonds in the N-CQDs, respectively (Wang et al., 2014a). In the spectra of C 1s and N 1s of BBN, the characteristic peaks of binding energy belonging to N-CQDs all existed and showed some slight shifts, which proves the interaction between N-CQDs and BO. Thus, it could be found from the BBN XPS spectrum that there were many chemical functional groups on the surface of BBN, which were consistent with the analysis of FTIR, indicating the N-CQDs adhered to the surface of BB through certain physical and chemical interactions (Gao et al., 2020).

To evaluate the optical properties of photocatalysts, the UV-Vis DRS measurement was performed in Fig. 4. In the inset of Fig. 4(a), pure BO and pure BOC appeared to be dark brown and bright white, whereas BB and BBN were in light brown color. Obviously, pure BOC presented a strong response in the UV region and the absorption edge was about 380 nm. The absorption edge of BO was located at about 900 nm, which played an intensive absorption in the visible and near-infrared (NIR) light region. The absorption edge of BB and BBN exhibited a red-shift compared with pure BOC. Especially for BBN, it displayed a great absorption response in the visible light region and this phenomenon demonstrated that coupling N-CQDs could effectively boost the visible-light harvesting efficiency, which further indicated that N-CQDs with excellent light absorption ability and up-conversion fluorescence performance promoted the visible-light capturing ability of BBN (Huang et al., 2017). Meanwhile, the bandgap of BO and BOC were calculated by Kubelka-Munk function to be 1.50 eV and 3.17 eV, respectively (Calculation details were in Text S6 based on the DRS results) (Wang et al., 2013).

The excitation-dependent PL performance of reed straw biomass N-CQDs under different excitation wavelengths was measured as exhibited in Fig. S8. The emission wavelength was red-shifted from 420 nm to 550 nm when the excitation wavelength increased from 300 nm to 440 nm, indicating the behavior of fluorescence was excitation-dependent (Guan et al., 2017). In addition, the strongest emission was located at 550 nm, while the excitation wavelength was fixed at 440 nm (Travlou et al., 2017). The intricate behavior was ascribed to the surface state and the size effect caused by the quantum dimension of as-synthesized N-CQDs (Zhu et al., 2013). Besides, the PL spectrum of BO, BOC, BB, and BBN were utilized to evaluate the charge transfer ability of photocatalysts (Fig. 4(b)). Obviously, BBN owned the weakest PL intensity illustrating that the decoration of N-CQDs would be more valid for the acceleration of interfacial charge transfer efficiency and further reflected the phenomenon about the photo-generated holes-electrons recombination decreased and the charge transfer promoted, which were beneficial to enhance the photocatalytic performance.

Furthermore, EIS and PCR analysis were conducted to investigate the charges generation and separation ability of BO, BOC, BB, and BBN under visible-light irradiation. As exhibited in Fig. 4(c), the Nyquist curve radius of BBN was smaller than that of other photocatalysts, indicating that the formation of heterojunction and introduction of N-CQDs made the as-prepared photocatalyst have higher interface charge transfer efficiency and lower migration resistance of carriers. The photocurrent density of all photocatalysts hardly changed in the five cycles under the visible-light irradiation (Fig. 4(d)), indicating all of them had stable visible-light response-ability. In particular, the photocurrent density of BBN was remarkably heightened which demonstrated the construction of BO and BOC heterojunction and the existence of N-CQDs had indeed solved the low electron utilization and high charge recombination rate problem of BO and BOC. To further investigate the impact of the formation of heterojunction on charge separation and transportability, the Mott-Schottky measurement was employed. In addition, from Fig. S9, the BBN exhibited the smallest slope of the plots than pure BO and BB, illustrating that BBN had an increased donor density (Wu et al., 2016a). Calculating from the results of Mott-Schottky, the carrier density (Nd) of BBN was 1.63 times and 1.27 times higher than that of BO and BB (the calculation details was in Text S7), which indicated that the formation of p-n heterojunction and the doping of N-CQDs could increase the carrier density evidently.

In order to evaluate the photocatalytic performance of the obtained photocatalysts, photodegradation experiments with SMX as the target pollutant was conducted. Fig. 5(a) illustrated that pure BOC had little influence on SMX photodegradation. About 63.03% of SMX was photodegraded within 30 min over BO, and it was improved to 79.56% after the formation of BB by the ion-etching method. Just like expected, because of the wide band gap of BOC, it was difficult to be excited to generate carriers under the visible light, thus, SMX was hard to be photodegraded. However, BBN performed excellent photocatalytic performance. The degradation rate of SMX could reach 96.48% within 30 min visible-light irradiation, proving that the incorporating of N-CQDs extremely enhanced the photocatalytic ability of BB. Specifically, the reason why the existence of N-CQDs could enhance catalytic performance was that N-CQDs could increase the visible-light absorption and store the photo-generated electrons to suppress the recombination of photo-generated charge carriers (Xie et al., 2021) Besides, the oxygen vacancies (OVs) of BO with abundant localized electrons would produce a defect state between the CB and VB of it (Yu et al., 2020; Z.W. Wang et al., 2020; J.J. Wang et al., 2020). Furthermore, when BBN was exposed to visible-light irradiation, the localized electrons of BBN would be excited to the CB, thereby prolonging the lifetime of photo-generated carriers to produce more reactive oxygen species, which was beneficial to the photodegradation of SMX (Yu et al., 2020). Meanwhile, compared to pure BO and BOC, composite BB and BBN owed larger specific surface area (23.582 m2 g−1 and 28.546 m2 g−1) and total pore volume (0.0972 cm3 g−1 and 0.1343 cm3 g−1), which could increase the contact opportunity with SMX molecules and further promoted the process of photodegradation. (The BET specific surface area (SBET), total pore volume, external surface area, and average pore diameter distribution of different materials were shown in Table S1).

In addition, the BBN system was applied to photodegradation of other six sulfonamides, including sulfathiazole (ST), sulfadiazine (SD), sulfamerazine (SMR), sulfamethazine (SM2), sulfamonomethoxine (SMM), and sulfadimethoxine (SDM), and the photodegradation rates of them were displayed in Fig. 5(b). Besides, the eco-toxicity evaluation of these SAs was evaluated by ECOSAR shown in Table S3. In the presence of BBN, the photodegradation efficiencies for the six sulfonamides were all above 95% within 30 min visible-light irradiation. Furthermore, the photodegradation activity of BBN is higher than most of the other bismuth-based photocatalysts reported previously ( Table 1), which demonstrated the BBN possessed perfect visible-light photocatalytic ability for the degradation of sulfonamide antibiotics.

The direct use of solar energy has always been a pivotal issue in the environmental field. Therefore, the photodegradation of SMX under natural sunlight was studied to evaluate the photocatalytic ability of BBN in the actual environment, and the optical power densities were measured as 48.2 mW/cm2 at that time. As illustrated in Fig. S10, the photodegradation rate of SMX by BBN within 30 min sunlight irradiation was 97.25%. Obviously, BBN presented an excellent degradation efficiency of SMX under natural sunlight irradiation, which adequately proved the potential for photocatalytic in practical application.

In general, the dosage of photocatalyst is one of the major factors affecting practical applications. Fig. S11 displayed the related influences of the dosage from 0.2 g/L to 1.0 g/L on SMX photodegradation. The degradation rate of SMX gradually strengthened to the maximum value of 96.48%, while the dosage increased from 0.2 g/L to 0.8 g/L. However, when the dosage of photocatalyst reached 1.0 g/L, the degradation efficiency had not been further increased. Similarly, the pseudo-first-order rate constant k ( k
0.8 g/L=0.1017 min−1 and k
1.0 g/L=0.0962 min−1) shown in Fig. S11 (b) also fitted this result. For low dosage, the active site was a limiting factor for SMX degradation, and as the dosage increased (0.2–0.8 g/L), the increased number of active sites accelerated the degradation of SMX. However, excessive dosage (1.0 g/L) caused light shielding and scattering, which would block the photodegradation process (Xie et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of inorganic anions and natural organic matter (NOM)
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of different concentrations for common anions (SO4 2-, Cl-, and NO3 -) and NOM (humic acid (HA), representing NOM in this work) existing in natural water on SMX photodegradation were studied. As displayed in Fig. 5(c, d), SO4 2- with different concentrations inhibited the photocatalytic activity of BBN to a large extent. After adding different concentrations (0.5 mM, 1 mM, and 2 mM) of SO4 2-, the SMX degradation rates were decreased from 96.48% to 45.56%, 49.3%, and 38.86%, respectively, which might be due to h+ could react with SO4 2- to form SO4·-, so that occupied some active sites of BBN (Yap et al., 2011). After the addition of Cl-, the SMX photodegradation rate also had a downward trend as the concentration increased. Previous studies pointed out that Cl- could compete for reactive species especially h+ and adsorb onto photocatalyst surface, thus affecting the photocatalytic performance (Duan et al., 2016). In terms of NO3 -, three kinds of concentrations had little effect on photodegradation, which might be due to the reformation of reactive species under visible-light irradiation when the NO3 - in the natural water (Sagi et al., 2015).

NOM as an essential component in water has a great effect on the migration and transformation of organic pollutants. The influence of HA with 5 mg/L, 10 mg/L, and 20 mg/L on SMX photodegradation was studied. In the BBN system, SMX degradation process was restrained at two lower concentrations and promoted at 20 mg/L HA addition, respectively. HA with low concentration inhibited the photocatalytic activity might relate to the existence of unsaturated functional groups, which could capture the reactive free radicals (Maoz et al., 2010). In addition, HA was a photosensitive organic matter in natural water which could easily be excited to generate reactive oxygen species such as ·O2 - to facilitate the photodegradation of SMX (Zepp et al., 1981).

To consider the possibility of practical application, the stability of BBN was carried out through the recycling experiment of SMX photodegradation under visible light. As displayed in Fig. S12(a), BBN showed excellent catalytic performance stability for SMX photodegradation after consecutive reactions five times. The photodegradation rates of SMX in the five rounds of the recycling experiment were 96.48%, 93.87%, 88.74%, 92.87%, and 85.38%, respectively. In addition, the used BBN-5 photocatalyst was characterized by XRD, FT-IR, and SEM to further demonstrate its stability (Fig. S12) (Zhang et al., 2022). It could be seen from the SEM image that the nanosheet part of the etched BOC was corroded, which might be one of the reasons for the decrease of XRD intensity. In addition, the FTIR spectra (Fig. S10 (c)) of BBN-5 had two distinct new peaks, the absorption at 1390 cm−1 was assigned to the C-N, probably because of the adsorption of SMX and the photodegradation products of it on the surface of BBN-5. And the peak at 846 cm−1 was due to the symmetric stretching vibration of Bi-O-Bi, representing the BiO3 unit, which might be caused by the exposure of BO after photodegradation (Devi et al., 2022). Although the chemical composition and surface morphology changed slightly compared with fresh BBN, the outstanding photodegradation efficiency and unchanged crystal structure of BBN-5 could further prove that BBN had excellent structural stability.

To quest the predominate free radicals of photodegradation of SMX by BBN, the benzoquinone (BQ), isopropanol (IPA), furfuryl alcohol (FFA), and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na) with the concentration of 0.1 mM were used as scavengers of superoxide radical (·O2 -), hydroxyl (·OH), singlet oxygen (1O2), and photo-generated holes (h+), respectively, and Fig. 6(a) displayed the results. The photodegradation rate of SMX was 96.48% within illumination 30 min without adding any scavengers. After adding BQ, IPA, FFA, and EDTA-2Na to the photocatalysis system, the photodegradation efficiencies of SMX reduced to 59.14%, 91.49%, 94.03%, and 9.13%, respectively, revealing ·O2 - and h+ were involved in the SMX photodegradation process, especially the inhibitory effect of h+. In order to study the changes of free radicals in the system after the formation of heterojunction in detail, the quenching experiments of BO were carried out. As demonstrated in Fig. S13, the SMX photodecomposition was significantly inhibited after adding the BQ, with the photodegradation efficiency obviously reduced from 63.03% to 24.87%, indicating that ·O2 - played a pivotal role in this process. The existence of IPA and EDTA-2Na had a negligible influence on the photodegradation of SMX. In particular, the photodegradation efficacy was slightly improved after adding the IPA, which might be because the band structure of BO could not produce ·OH under the visible light irradiation (explain in detail in the following section) and IPA could act as electron donor and promote the formation of ·O2 - (Ganadu et al., 2002). Meanwhile, the h+ generated by the light excitation of BO had a weak oxidizing ability, resulting in little effect on the photodegradation of SMX, which was in sharp contrast with the BBN system.

Moreover, the ESR technology was utilized to verify the existence of ·O2 -, ·OH, 1O2, and h+. From Fig. 6(b), the existence of DMPO-·O2 - signals illustrated the creation of ·O2 - in the BBN photocatalysis system and it also showed that BBN did not produce ·OH under visible-light irradiation. In addition, under the visible light irradiation, the peak of 1O2 with high intensity employing 2,2,6,6-Tetramethyl-4-piperidone hydrochloride (TEMP) as the trapping agent. However, in the BQ solution, the intensity of the 1O2 signal was significantly reduced, indicating that the generation of 1O2 was related to superoxide radicals, which was consistent with the previous report that ·O2 - could be transformed to 1O2 by h+ oxidation (Zhang et al., 2021). Furthermore, three obvious signals belonging to TEMPO-h+ were observed in the dark and decreased with the irradiation (Chen et al., 2020). All the EPR curves illustrated the generation of active species among the process of photodegradation, which was consistent with the result of the quenching experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The flat potentials calculation of BO and BOC
ce:section.ce:para: Moreover, the Mott-Schottky plots of BO and BOC were revealed in Fig. 6(c, d). The flat potentials of BO and BOC were calculated to − 0.7 V and 2.51 V vs. Ag/AgCl electrode, respectively. The potential vs. Ag/AgCl electrode was converted to the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) scale using ENHE = EAg/AgCl + E0 (E0 = 0.197 V). Thence, BO and BOC were equivalent to − 0.50 V and 2.71 V vs. NHE. The Mott-Schottky curve of BO and BOC exhibited positive and negative slopes, respectively, demonstrating that BO was an n-type semiconductor whereas BOC was a p-type semiconductor. The CB potential of n-type semiconductor considered to be more negative than that of flat-potential by about 0.2 V, while the VB potential of p-type semiconductor was more positive than that of flat-potential by about 0.2 V (Zou et al., 2019). Therefore, the CB of BO and the VB of BOC were calculated to be − 0.7 V and 2.9 V vs. NHE, respectively. Based on the results of the band-gap from DRS analysis, the VB for BO and the CB for BOC were evaluated to be 0.8 V and − 0.27 V vs. NHE by the equation ECB=EVB-Eg. Similarly, the CB and VB of BBN could be calculated according to the above method to be − 0.15 eV vs. NHE and 2.15 eV vs. NHE, respectively (The M-S curve and the Eg of BBN were shown in Fig. S14).

The DFT calculation was employed to investigate the effect of the BO/BOC and introduced N-CQDs on the promoted photocatalytic performance. Fig. 7(a-c) illustrated the total/partial density of states (TDOS/PDOS) of BO, BOC, and BBN. The CB and VB of BO were mostly composed of Bi 6p orbits and O 2p orbits showing the half-metallic characteristic owing to the small band-gap and the low hybridization between Bi atoms and adjacent O atoms in VB, thence, the VB electrons were easy to be excited (Jia et al., 2019). However, the huge hybrid electron density of O atoms with Bi atoms around the CB resulted in high recombination of photo-generated carriers, which performed at the most CB electrons transferred inside BO. For BOC, the CB was composed of Bi 6p, O 2p, and Cl 3p orbits while the VB was mainly originated from Bi 6p and O 2p orbits. The VB electrons of BOC were difficult to be excited because of the wide bandgap of BOC, corresponding to the results of DRS. In addition, due to the low hybridization, the CB electrons could be excited easily and migrated to the surface of BOC. It was noteworthy to mention that the TDOS at the Fermi level in the BBN photocatalytic was significantly larger than that of BO and BOC, illustrating that BBN possessed higher carrier mobility (Li et al., 2019). The high conductivity of BBN could favor the separation of carriers and further improve the photocatalytic reactions between SMX and the active species.

Then, the charge density difference of BBN was described in Fig. 7(d, e). Obviously, the difference in charge density chiefly occurred at the interface of N-CQDs/BO/BOC and BO/BOC. The blue and yellow regions represented the electron depletion and accumulation, respectively, demonstrating that the photo-generated electrons could transfer from BO to BOC in the heterojunction. Besides, there were obvious electrons accumulation and depletion phenomena around isolated N-CQDs, which illustrated N-CQDs could promote photo-generated charge transfer as electrons acceptor and donor, manifesting that the charge redistribution around the doped N-CQDs accelerates the separation of electrons-holes (Miao et al., 2016).

The BBN photocatalyst was shown to achieve the interfacial charge carriers transfer and improve photocatalytic performance. The work functions were calculated to better study the heterojunction formed between BO nanorod (1 0 0) planes and BOC nanosheet (0 0 1) planes, and the work functions of them were estimated to be 7.05 eV and 7.49 eV displayed in Fig. 8(a-b), respectively, which indicated the Fermi level of BO was higher than BOC. In these cases, when the BO contacted BOC, the electrons in BO could flow to BOC until the Fermi level reached equilibrium, resulting in a positive charge on the n-type BO and a negative charge on the p-type BOC (Fig. 8(c and d)) (Liu et al., 2016). An internal electrostatic field directed from the BO to BOC was generated due to the process of separation of charges. As a result, under the visible-light irradiation, the photo-excited electrons on the VB of BOC were spontaneously migrated to the CB of BO driven by the excited electric field. Meanwhile, biomass N-CQDs as a receptor and donor of electrons accelerated the shuttle of electrons on the CB of BOC to the CB of BO, causing an effective promotion of the photo-generated charge separation, and then simultaneously increased the charge transfer in BOC. Besides, combining the results of the quenching experiment with BO and BBN, the accumulated electrons on BO reacted with O2 in the aqueous to produce ·O2 - radicals and further oxidized SMX to be degradation products. And the photo-generated h+ situated in the VB of BO could migrate to the VB of BOC, which could directly mineralize SMX. But for the single BO photocatalytic system, because the valence band of BO was relatively negative, the oxidation ability of h+ on the VB of BO generated by visible light excitation was weak, resulting in the lower photocatalytic degradation ability of SMX. Moreover, in addition to being an acceptor and donor for photo-generated electrons to promote charges transfer, N-CQDs also had great effects on enhancing visible-light absorption due to the introduction of the amino group (-NH2). And the oxygen vacancies of BO also could boost the electron donation ability in BBN by separating the photo-generated e--h+ pairs. Thence, the excellent photocatalytic capability of BBN photocatalyst could be ascribed to the synergistic effect of accelerated separation of e--h+ in p-n heterojunction and the electron-rich performance of N-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible photodegradation pathway and mineralization activity
ce:section.ce:para: Using HRMS to detect and analyze the photodegradation products of SMX. The dominant pathway of SMX photodegradation over BBN under the visible-light irradiation contained hydroxylation, oxidation, substitution, and bond breakage. As displayed in Scheme 1, SMX would be converted to 4-amino-3, 5-dihydroxy-N-(5-methylisoxazol-3-yl)-benzenesulfonamide (P1, m/z = 286) and 4-amino-3-dihydroxy-N-(5-methylisoxazol-3-yl)-benzenesulfonamide (P2, m/z = 271) by hydroxylation (Xie et al., 2019). In addition, the oxidation of amino group on the benzene ring in SMX to form N-(5-methylisoxazol-3-yl)− 4-nitrobenzenesulfonamide (P3, m/z = 283). And the photodegradation product 4-hydroxy-N-(5-methylisoxazol-3-yl)-benzenesulfonamide (P4, m/z = 254) might attribute to the substitution reaction. Meanwhile, bond breakage was also the pivotal process in the photodegradation of SMX, including the breaking S-N, C-N, and S-C bonds. 4-aminobenzenesulfonic acid (P5, m/z = 173) generated by S-N bond breaking through reactive species attacking, then it would be further oxidized and deaminated to product 4-nitrobenzenesulfonic acid (P6, m/z = 204) and benzenesulfonic acid (P7, m/z = 158). 4-aminobenzenesulfonamide (P8, m/z = 172) might derive from the rupture of the C-N bond, which was in line with previous research (Zhu et al., 2019). In the presence of active species, the S-C bond was split to form P9 (m/z = 178) and aniline (P10, m/z = 93, undetected), then 2-aminobenzene-1, 3-diol (P11, m/z = 111), and hydroquinone (P12, m/z = 125) would yield by the hydroxylation of undetected intermediates P10. Furthermore, under the action of ·O2 - and h+, SMX experienced aromatic ring breaking and then form ring-opening products, CO2, H2O, and some inorganic ions, which would be verified by the TOC removal rate later.

3D EEM was detected to analyze the changes of SMX in the photodegradation process. Six samples after BBN photolysis were collected after visible-light irradiation at 0 min, 5 min, 10 min, 30 min, 60 min, and 120 min. As presented in Fig. 9(a-f), the fluorescence peak of SMX was identified at λEx/λEm = (225–300 nm)/(300–400 nm) (Jiang et al., 2019). With the increase of visible-light irradiation time, the peak intensity reduced rapidly, which was ascribed to the decrease in the concentration of SMX. Obviously, after 30 min visible-light illumination, the fluorescence peak of SMX disappeared, indicating SMX was photodegraded and transformed to non-fluorescence substances. In addition, total organic carbon (TOC) was monitored to accurately quantify the mineralization degrees for SMX. As demonstrated in Fig. 9(g), after 30 min photocatalytic reaction, the TOC removal rate was 29.73%, and further extending the illumination time to 120 min, the TOC removal efficiency of BBN could reach 89.4%, it was reasonable to infer that SMX could be mineralized completely by extending photocatalytic reaction time.

In addition, in order to determine the latent environmental influence of SMX and photodegradation intermediate formed by the BBN photocatalytic system, the toxicity of them were assessed by utilizing the ECOSAR program. As illustrated in Fig. S15, the acute toxicity and chronic toxicity of most of the photodegradation intermediate were lower than those of SMX. Although the eco-toxicity of several intermediates (P2, P8, and P11 for daphnid) was slightly higher than that of the target pollutant SMX, the concentrations of them were limited and they would be further degraded by photocatalysis to form uncomplicated non-toxic compounds, such as CO2 and H2O. Thence, the above results illustrated that the final products of SMX photodegradation through the BBN system might not be noxious to the aquatic organisms, which have a tremendous significance on the safety of the aquatic environment.

The evaluation of electrical energy consumption in the photocatalytic process is also the most important consideration in its practical application. The International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) proposed electrical energy per order (EEO) to illustrate the sustainability and efficiency of advanced oxidation technology. Thence, evaluating the energy utilization of BBN for SMX photodegradation under the visible-light irradiation by LED lamp, and the EEO is calculated through the following equation (Lee et al., 2018):
(1)
E
Eo
=
38.4 * P
V * k
Where
P , V , and k
are the lamp power (kW), the volume of SMX solution (m3), and the pseudo-first-order rate constant (min−1), respectively. The EEO of different photocatalysts for SMX degradation was demonstrated in Table S4. It was obviously concluded that BBN (EEO=378) was the supreme efficient composite for photodegradation of SMX compared to other photocatalysts.

In this study, novel, efficient, and environmental-friendly photocatalyst BiO2−x/BiOCl with p-n heterojunction decorated by straw biomass N-CQDs was successfully synthesized by the facile two-step methods. Compared with pure BiO2−x and BiOCl, the heterojunction photocatalyst BBN exhibited higher carrier concentration, high photodegradation efficiency of SMX (96.48%) under the visible-light irradiation, and had an excellent photodegradation efficiency for other sulfonamide antibiotics. Besides, the enhanced photocatalytic ability resulting in the promoted generation of ·O2 -, reduced the toxicity of photodegradation product (by ESACOR program), and high mineralization rate of SMX (by TOC and 3D EEM analysis). Moreover, combined experimental data, DFT calculation, and optical properties clearly demonstrated the mechanism of the improved photocatalytic performance, which explained in detail the electrons transfer mechanism induced by the internal electronic field at the interface of N-CQDs, n-type BiO2−x, and p-type BiOCl. Finally, the evaluation of energy utilization illustrated that BBN had a comparatively smaller EEO (EEO=378) than other photocatalysts for SMX photodegradation, showing that BBN photocatalysis process was more energy-saving than others. In summary, the efficient and green BBN could provide a better prospect for degrading SAs from water under visible-light irradiation.

Kemin Qi: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Experiment, Data curation, Writing – original draft preparation. Yuping Ye: Software, Experiment, Investigation. Bin Wei: Software, Experiment. Mengxin Li: Experiment, Software. Yanxin Lun: Experiment. Xiaoyun Xie: Resources, Supervision, Conceptualization, Validation, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Haijiao Xie: DFT calculation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 42077330).

ce:section.ce:para: In this work, we prepared a p-n heterojunction photocatalyst BiO2−x/BiOCl decorated with N-doped biomass carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs). The mechanism of electrons transfers on the N-CQDs/BiO2−x/BiOCl was elaborated by the density functional theory(DFT) calculation. Quenching experiments and EPR analyses uncovered that the ·O2 - and h+ were the essential active species of sulfamethoxazole photodegradation because N-CQDs could gather electrons to validly inhibit the recombination of e--h+. Furthermore, the small electrical energy per order (EEO=378) indicated this photocatalysis process was energy-saving. Overall, we believe that the economical and green N-CQDs/BiO2−x/BiOCl could deliver more fit-for-purpose and sustainable solutions toward PPCPs-contaminated wastewater treatment.
